cases.title: Avery and Lewelling v. Nashville Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: RE John Avery and RE Dale Lewelling

cases.synopsis: Avery and Lewelling complained against Nashville Presbytery's denial of their complaint about the Shepherding Committee's investigation of TE Jim Bachmann. The SJC dismissed the complaint as judicially out of order because the actions complained of were those of a committee rather than a court, and the complaint against November 2015 Presbytery action was untimely under BCO 43-2.

cases.title: Doty v. Nashville Presbytery
cases.synopsis: Ms. Doty and 21 others filed charges against a Nashville Presbytery teaching elder first with his congregation's Session (wrong court), then with Nashville Presbytery; Presbytery declined to indict and treated the matter as a report under BCO 31-2 rather than charges. The SJC dismissed the complaint as judicially out of order (11-9-2-2) on the ground that Doty lacked standing under BCO 43-1 as a non-member of Nashville Presbytery; a significant dissent argued that her filing of charges gave her standing under prior precedent (Lyons, Ruff, Fordice).

cases.title: Fordice v. Pacific Northwest Presbytery
precedent_refs: Hutchinson & Bulkeley v. Western Carolina Presbytery
cases.synopsis: Fordice complained that Pacific Northwest Presbytery erred by allowing TE Nathan Lewis to confess under BCO 38-1 and closing the matter without adequate process, after its own investigative committee had found a strong presumption of guilt on 18 allegations. The SJC unanimously sustained the complaint and remanded the case to PNW, finding that while use of BCO 38-1 was not itself error, the approved statement of facts was not 'full' as required because it failed to correlate with the underlying allegations.

cases.title: Harwell et al. v. Nashville Presbytery

parties.complainant_or_appellant: RE Sam K. Harwell, et al.

cases.synopsis: The SJC dismissed this complaint as judicially out of order because the Harwell complainants had not first properly filed a complaint with Nashville Presbytery—what they filed with Presbytery was in fact a suggestion of charges or call for investigation under BCO 31-2, not a complaint against a court action—and then filed their SJC complaint directly, bypassing the required step of complaining first to the court whose action is alleged to be in error.

cases.title: Wills v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE Tolivar Wills complained that Metro Atlanta Presbytery improperly dissolved Grace North Atlanta Church without following formal judicial process under BCO 40-6. The SJC denied the complaint, ruling that BCO 13-9(f) grants presbytery inherent authority to dissolve a church without mandating any specific procedure, and that the record did not demonstrate clear error in MAP's exercise of its discretion and judgment.
